titleStmt.title: Letter from May Fay to James Finn, 19 January 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: May Fay

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from May Fay (1897-1976) to James Finn (1877-1922) informing James about her latest correspondence from friends and family about their recent engagement. May expresses her happiness that James would choose to be with her but worries over her own inadequacies. Before signing off May tells James about her recent illness.May Fay became engaged to James Finn in January 1916. Despite the twenty year age gap their letters show a very personal and private insight into courting in early 20th century Ireland. After her husband's death May held on to his letters as her reminder of him. May never remarried and focused her attention instead on her businesses in order to provide for their children.James Finn was a civil servant, working in the National Health Service Commission on Mount Street. While neither James nor May were politically active their letters provide a very unique insight into the affairs of Easter Week and its aftermath. James died in 1922 as a result of the flu epidemic, May was pregnant with their fourth child at the time.

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Togherstown Ballinea 19√¢¬Ä¬î1√¢¬Ä¬î16 My dearest James Your nice letter
was indeed very welcome
This morn I was watching
out for it but had long
given up when Paddie arrived
about 12:30 we were expecting
a line from Mother, but did
get one still I suppose she
is safe or we would hear
before this. I had a very nice
letter from Moll although
I have never had the pleasure
of meeting her It was very
good of her to write. Oh Joe
Wynne wrote me saying how delighted she was about
my engagement & what a lucky
girl I was, for myself I cannot
imagine even yet one like
you could be content with
a plain country girl of course
The thought that you are realy
fond of me makes me very
very happy but I cannot
help letting my worthlessness
in every way rest very heavily
on my mind.
About going to town I see now
it was all for the best too allowed Mother
off first had we decided on
taking the longed for visit
on Sat I certainly would be left
The cold I had the day you
left which seemed nothing
proved much worse than
I even knew it did until last
night I was very restless
coughing smothering etc.
I remained in bed till very
late today but Thank God I
feel quiet on the mend now
I am managing to keep
the 'blossoms' off my lips &
nose by means of Corbetts
ointment so I expect I will
feel realy fit by the time
Mother comes home Tommy
Derry spent last night
with us his Mother being
in B. More he said it
was a 'Gods Charity' I had
no one to do anything for
my cold whas made I
leave my own road &
he always so nice to me
Mrs S. says every one in
there is delighted with the
news Josephine wrote
me good wishes thro'
Mrs. Seery would have sent
wires to us both but was
afraid of being laughed at Mrs. S says she is very
jealous at not hearing from
you but sends warmest love
I was in town (on 9th) Sat
last I payed a long promised
visit to Brother Casey I was very glad to get
out of him nothwithsanding
how nice he was to me
took me on knee put his
arm round my neck
in fact did every thing but
kiss me I was going to tell
him Id tell you but thought
it better not. What you said of me
in the end of your letter did
indeed make me laugh &
laugh hearty I was going
to tell Nano what I was
laughing at but I knew drunkness
would be her first thought Bye Bye now dearest James lots of love from your own May
